risk_findings.title: [attack-lab/cluster-admin-sa] ServiceAccount can create ClusterRoleBindings — cluster-wide privilege escalation (no active foothold)
risk_findings.description: ServiceAccount "cluster-admin-sa" in namespace "attack-lab" can create ClusterRoleBindings, enabling binding to cluster-admin or any ClusterRole. Foothold: no running workloads.
evidence: system:serviceaccount:attack-lab:cluster-admin-sa can create ClusterRoleBindings, enabling binding to cluster-admin or any ClusterRole — bound via: ClusterRole/cluster-admin (via cluster-admin-binding)
No running pods or workloads use this ServiceAccount — privilege exists but there is no direct execution foothold.
risk_findings.mitigation: Remove create/patch on clusterrolebindings from this SA. Restrict to break-glass accounts only.

risk_findings.title: [attack-lab/cluster-admin-sa] ServiceAccount can create RoleBindings — namespace privilege escalation (no active foothold)
risk_findings.description: ServiceAccount "cluster-admin-sa" in namespace "attack-lab" can create RoleBindings, enabling namespace-scoped privilege escalation. Foothold: no running workloads.
evidence: system:serviceaccount:attack-lab:cluster-admin-sa can create RoleBindings, enabling namespace-scoped privilege escalation — bound via: ClusterRole/cluster-admin (via cluster-admin-binding)
No running pods or workloads use this ServiceAccount — privilege exists but there is no direct execution foothold.
risk_findings.mitigation: Restrict RoleBinding creation to dedicated CI/CD service accounts with audit alerting.

risk_findings.title: [attack-lab/cluster-admin-sa] ServiceAccount can impersonate users/SAs — identity takeover (no active foothold)
risk_findings.description: ServiceAccount "cluster-admin-sa" in namespace "attack-lab" has impersonate permission, allowing it to act as any other user or service account. Foothold: no running workloads.
evidence: system:serviceaccount:attack-lab:cluster-admin-sa has impersonate permission, allowing it to act as any other user or service account — bound via: ClusterRole/cluster-admin (via cluster-admin-binding)
No running pods or workloads use this ServiceAccount — privilege exists but there is no direct execution foothold.
risk_findings.mitigation: The impersonate verb should only be held by aggregated API server accounts. Remove immediately.

risk_findings.title: [attack-lab/cluster-admin-sa] ServiceAccount can patch ClusterRoles — arbitrary permission escalation (no active foothold)
risk_findings.description: ServiceAccount "cluster-admin-sa" in namespace "attack-lab" can patch ClusterRole definitions, adding arbitrary rules to any existing cluster role. Foothold: no running workloads.
evidence: system:serviceaccount:attack-lab:cluster-admin-sa can patch ClusterRole definitions, adding arbitrary rules to any existing cluster role — bound via: ClusterRole/cluster-admin (via cluster-admin-binding)
No running pods or workloads use this ServiceAccount — privilege exists but there is no direct execution foothold.
risk_findings.mitigation: Treat ClusterRole patch as equivalent to cluster-admin. Remove from non-platform-team accounts.

risk_findings.title: [attack-lab/cluster-admin-sa] ServiceAccount can create SA tokens — long-lived credential generation (no active foothold)
risk_findings.description: ServiceAccount "cluster-admin-sa" in namespace "attack-lab" can generate ServiceAccount tokens for any SA, producing long-lived impersonation credentials. Foothold: no running workloads.
evidence: system:serviceaccount:attack-lab:cluster-admin-sa can generate ServiceAccount tokens for any SA, producing long-lived impersonation credentials — bound via: ClusterRole/cluster-admin (via cluster-admin-binding)
No running pods or workloads use this ServiceAccount — privilege exists but there is no direct execution foothold.
risk_findings.mitigation: Restrict serviceaccounts/token create to operator accounts. Prefer projected volumes.

risk_findings.title: [attack-lab/cluster-admin-sa] ServiceAccount can get/read Secret values (no active foothold)
risk_findings.description: ServiceAccount "cluster-admin-sa" in namespace "attack-lab" can read raw Secret values (tokens, passwords, certificates) via GET. Foothold: no running workloads.
evidence: system:serviceaccount:attack-lab:cluster-admin-sa can read raw Secret values (tokens, passwords, certificates) via GET — bound via: ClusterRole/cluster-admin (via cluster-admin-binding)
No running pods or workloads use this ServiceAccount — privilege exists but there is no direct execution foothold.
risk_findings.mitigation: Restrict secret get to specific resourceNames. Migrate to external secret stores.

risk_findings.title: [attack-lab/cluster-admin-sa] ServiceAccount can list Secrets (no active foothold)
risk_findings.description: ServiceAccount "cluster-admin-sa" in namespace "attack-lab" can enumerate all Secret names in accessible namespaces. Foothold: no running workloads.
evidence: system:serviceaccount:attack-lab:cluster-admin-sa can enumerate all Secret names in accessible namespaces — bound via: ClusterRole/cluster-admin (via cluster-admin-binding)
No running pods or workloads use this ServiceAccount — privilege exists but there is no direct execution foothold.
risk_findings.mitigation: Restrict Secret list to specific resourceNames. Enable audit logging for list events.

risk_findings.title: [attack-lab/cluster-admin-sa] ServiceAccount can patch Deployments — workload takeover / SA lateral movement (no active foothold)
risk_findings.description: ServiceAccount "cluster-admin-sa" in namespace "attack-lab" can patch Deployments, injecting containers, changing service accounts, or replacing images. Foothold: no running workloads.
evidence: system:serviceaccount:attack-lab:cluster-admin-sa can patch Deployments, injecting containers, changing service accounts, or replacing images — bound via: ClusterRole/cluster-admin (via cluster-admin-binding)
No running pods or workloads use this ServiceAccount — privilege exists but there is no direct execution foothold.
risk_findings.mitigation: Limit deployment patch to dedicated CD service accounts. Use Kyverno/OPA for pod spec validation.

risk_findings.title: [attack-lab/cluster-admin-sa] ServiceAccount can patch DaemonSets — node-level code execution (no active foothold)
risk_findings.description: ServiceAccount "cluster-admin-sa" in namespace "attack-lab" can patch DaemonSets; changes roll out to every node in the cluster simultaneously. Foothold: no running workloads.
evidence: system:serviceaccount:attack-lab:cluster-admin-sa can patch DaemonSets; changes roll out to every node in the cluster simultaneously — bound via: ClusterRole/cluster-admin (via cluster-admin-binding)
No running pods or workloads use this ServiceAccount — privilege exists but there is no direct execution foothold.
risk_findings.mitigation: Strictly limit DaemonSet modifications. Apply Pod Security Admission baseline/restricted.

risk_findings.title: [attack-lab/cluster-admin-sa] ServiceAccount can create Pods — potential node escape if PSA is permissive (no active foothold)

evidence: system:serviceaccount:attack-lab:cluster-admin-sa can create Pods; with a permissive PSA profile this enables privileged pod / node escape — bound via: ClusterRole/cluster-admin (via cluster-admin-binding)

risk_findings.mitigation: Enforce Pod Security Admission at 'restricted' level. Block hostPID/hostNetwork via OPA.

risk_findings.title: [attack-lab/cluster-admin-sa] ServiceAccount can exec into Pods — direct container shell access (no active foothold)

risk_findings.title: Privileged containers detected in cluster

Pod kube-system/kube-proxy-q6ksq has privileged containers: [kube-proxy]
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Replace privileged containers with specific Linux capabilities (CAP_NET_ADMIN etc.) as needed.

• Audit why each privileged container requires elevated access and minimize.
• Use Falco to detect privileged container activity at runtime.

risk_findings.title: HostPath volumes detected — host filesystem exposure
risk_findings.description: 8 workload(s)/pod(s) mount sensitive hostPath volumes. These grant containers read/write access to host filesystem paths, enabling credential theft or container escape.
evidence: workload:kube-system:kube-proxy mounts "/run/xtables.lock" [HIGH: sensitive host path]
pod:attack-lab:privileged-pod mounts "/" [HIGH: sensitive host path]
pod:kube-system:kube-apiserver-minikube mounts "/etc/ssl/certs" [HIGH: sensitive host path]
pod:kube-system:kube-apiserver-minikube mounts "/etc/ca-certificates" [HIGH: sensitive host path]
pod:kube-system:kube-controller-manager-minikube mounts "/etc/ssl/certs" [HIGH: sensitive host path]
pod:kube-system:kube-controller-manager-minikube mounts "/etc/ca-certificates" [HIGH: sensitive host path]
pod:kube-system:kube-controller-manager-minikube mounts "/etc/kubernetes/controller-manager.conf" [CRITICAL: cluster config → credential theft]
pod:kube-system:kube-proxy-q6ksq mounts "/run/xtables.lock" [HIGH: sensitive host path]
pod:kube-system:kube-scheduler-minikube mounts "/etc/kubernetes/scheduler.conf" [CRITICAL: cluster config → credential theft]
pod:monitoring:log-collector mounts "/var/run/docker.sock" [CRITICAL: Docker socket → container escape]
pod:monitoring:metrics-exporter mounts "/proc" [HIGH: sensitive host path]
pod:monitoring:metrics-exporter mounts "/sys" [HIGH: sensitive host path]
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Replace hostPath mounts with PersistentVolumeClaims.
• If hostPath is required, use readOnly=true and restrict to the minimum path.
• Block hostPath via OPA/Gatekeeper or Kyverno policy.
• Especially dangerous paths: /, /etc, /var/run/docker.sock, /run/containerd, /etc/kubernetes/pki.

risk_findings.title: Pods using host PID/Network namespace — container isolation bypass
risk_findings.description: Workloads are sharing host PID or network namespaces, significantly reducing container isolation and enabling attacks against host processes.
evidence: Workload kube-system/kube-proxy: hostNetwork
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Remove hostPID and hostNetwork from all workloads that don't require them.
• hostPID allows process inspection/signal of all host processes.
• hostNetwork exposes the host network stack and can bypass NetworkPolicies.
• Enforce via Pod Security Admission 'restricted' policy.

Role /system:controller:generic-garbage-collector has wildcard: verbs=[delete get list patch update watch] resources=[*]
Role /system:controller:horizontal-pod-autoscaler has wildcard: verbs=[get list watch] resources=[*]
Role /system:controller:horizontal-pod-autoscaler has wildcard: verbs=[get list watch] resources=[*]
Role /system:controller:namespace-controller has wildcard: verbs=[delete deletecollection get list watch] resources=[*]
Role /system:controller:resourcequota-controller has wildcard: verbs=[list watch] resources=[*]
Role /system:kube-controller-manager has wildcard: verbs=[list watch] resources=[*]
Role /system:kubelet-api-admin has wildcard: verbs=[*] resources=[nodes/log nodes/metrics nodes/proxy nodes/stats]
Role /system:kubelet-api-admin has wildcard: verbs=[*] resources=[nodes/configz nodes/healthz nodes/pods]
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Replace '*' verbs/resources with explicit lists following least-privilege.
• Audit each ClusterRole/Role with wildcards and replace with minimal permission sets.
• Use 'kubectl auth reconcile' to diff and apply minimal RBAC manifests.

risk_findings.title: Non-system identity bound to cluster-admin ClusterRole
risk_findings.description: Non-system identities are directly bound to the cluster-admin ClusterRole. These identities have unrestricted access to all cluster resources.
evidence: CRB "cluster-admin-binding" binds ServiceAccount "cluster-admin-sa" to cluster-admin
CRB "minikube-rbac" binds ServiceAccount "default" to cluster-admin
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Immediately review and remove non-emergency cluster-admin bindings.
• Use namespace-scoped admin roles instead of cluster-admin wherever possible.
• Implement MPA or GitOps approval workflows for cluster-admin binding changes.
• Enable audit alerting on any cluster-admin role use.

risk_findings.title: Secrets exposed as environment variables in workloads
risk_findings.description: 1 workload(s) inject secrets as environment variables. Env vars are accessible to any process in the container, appear in process listings, and may leak into logs or crash reports.
evidence: prod/payment-service [payments]: env var "STRIPE_SECRET_KEY" → secret "api-keys" key "stripe_key"
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Prefer volume mounts over environment variables for secrets — env vars appear in process
  listings (/proc/<pid>/environ), crash dumps, debug logs, and kubectl describe output.
• Use external secret stores (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, ESO) with sidecar injection.
• If env vars are unavoidable, scope the secret to specific resourceNames in RBAC.
• Rotate secrets that have been visible as env vars in case of prior exposure.

risk_findings.title: Workloads with automountServiceAccountToken not explicitly disabled
risk_findings.description: 5 workload(s) automatically mount a ServiceAccount token. If these workloads don't need API access, the token is unnecessary attack surface.
evidence: Workload kube-system/coredns does not disable automountServiceAccountToken
Workload kube-system/kube-proxy does not disable automountServiceAccountToken
Workload monitoring/prometheus does not disable automountServiceAccountToken
Workload prod/cloud-app does not disable automountServiceAccountToken
Workload prod/payment-service does not disable automountServiceAccountToken
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Set automountServiceAccountToken: false on all pods/SAs that don't need API access.
• This prevents the SA token from being injected into the pod filesystem, reducing SSRF blast radius.

risk_findings.title: ServiceAccount with cloud IAM role annotation — cross-cloud escalation path
risk_findings.description: 1 ServiceAccount(s) carry cloud IAM role annotations. Any workload running as these SAs can call cloud APIs (S3, IAM, KMS, etc.) using the pod's projected service account token — no secrets required. Compromising these pods enables cross-cloud privilege escalation.
evidence: [AWS] SA prod/cloud-sa → AWS cloud role "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/prod-s3-admin"
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Audit all cloud IAM roles bound to Kubernetes service accounts.
• Apply least-privilege IAM policies to IRSA/Workload-Identity roles.
• Use IAM Conditions (AWS: aws:RequestedRegion, GCP: resource.name) to limit scope.
• Enable CloudTrail/Cloud Audit Logs for actions performed via workload identity.
• Revoke unused IRSA roles immediately; any pod running as this SA can call cloud APIs.

risk_findings.title: Identity can patch MutatingWebhookConfigurations → intercept any pod
risk_findings.description: The current identity can modify MutatingWebhookConfigurations. An attacker can redirect admission requests to a rogue HTTPS endpoint, mutating any new pod spec to inject malicious containers, steal SA tokens, or add environment variables with exfiltration payloads. Attack: patch the webhook to point to attacker.example.com, then wait for pod creation.
evidence: SSRR: patch/create mutatingwebhookconfigurations in "monitoring"
SSRR: patch/create mutatingwebhookconfigurations in "prod"
SSRR: patch/create mutatingwebhookconfigurations in "attack-lab"
SSRR: patch/create mutatingwebhookconfigurations in "default"
SSRR: patch/create mutatingwebhookconfigurations in "kube-node-lease"
SSRR: patch/create mutatingwebhookconfigurations in "kube-public"
SSRR: patch/create mutatingwebhookconfigurations in "kube-system"
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Patching MutatingWebhookConfigurations allows redirecting all admission requests to an attacker-controlled endpoint.
• Restrict patch/create on mutatingwebhookconfigurations to platform-team break-glass accounts only.
• Use OPA/Gatekeeper to require webhook configurations to point to approved service endpoints.
• Alert on any change to MutatingWebhookConfigurations via audit policy.

risk_findings.title: Projected SA tokens with non-Kubernetes audience — usable outside cluster
risk_findings.description: 2 workload(s)/pod(s) mount projected ServiceAccount tokens with non-Kubernetes audiences. These tokens are signed by the cluster but accepted by external services (AWS STS, Vault, etc.). A container compromise yields a token usable outside the cluster.
evidence: workload:prod:cloud-app: projected SA token with audience "sts.amazonaws.com" (usable by external service "sts.amazonaws.com")
pod:prod:cloud-app-f7fdb9fdd-h95x8: projected SA token with audience "sts.amazonaws.com" (usable by external service "sts.amazonaws.com")
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Projected tokens with custom audiences are designed for external service authentication.
• Ensure only intended services can receive these tokens (verify audience consumers).
• Apply short expiration times (ExpirationSeconds) to projected tokens.
• Audit which workloads mount non-standard audience tokens and verify necessity.

risk_findings.title: Critical hostPath + exec access → node-level compromise path
risk_findings.description: CRITICAL: The current identity can exec into 2 pod(s) that mount critical host paths (/etc/kubernetes/pki, /var/lib/kubelet). Attack: exec into pod → read kubelet client cert or cluster CA key → forge credentials for any cluster identity.
evidence: pod:kube-system:kube-controller-manager-minikube mounts "/etc/kubernetes/controller-manager.conf" AND identity can exec → exec in → read node credentials
pod:kube-system:kube-scheduler-minikube mounts "/etc/kubernetes/scheduler.conf" AND identity can exec → exec in → read node credentials
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Remove hostPath mounts to critical paths (/etc/kubernetes, /var/lib/kubelet, /etc/kubernetes/pki).
• Restrict pods/exec to break-glass accounts only; use ephemeral containers for debugging.
• Enable Pod Security Admission 'restricted' to block hostPath on production workloads.
• Use Falco to detect exec into pods that have critical hostPath mounts.

risk_findings.title: Identity can use nodes/proxy subresource — direct kubelet API access
risk_findings.description: The current identity can use the nodes/proxy subresource, which tunnels arbitrary HTTP requests directly to the kubelet API. This bypasses RBAC for kubelet endpoints, enabling attackers to read pod logs, execute commands, and access node-level secrets.
evidence: SSRR: nodes/proxy access in namespace "default"
SSRR: nodes/proxy access in namespace "kube-node-lease"
SSRR: nodes/proxy access in namespace "kube-public"
SSRR: nodes/proxy access in namespace "kube-system"
SSRR: nodes/proxy access in namespace "monitoring"
SSRR: nodes/proxy access in namespace "prod"
SSRR: nodes/proxy access in namespace "attack-lab"
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Remove nodes/proxy from all non-system service accounts.
• nodes/proxy allows direct, unauthenticated-from-kubelet-perspective requests that bypass RBAC on the kubelet.
• Enable NodeRestriction admission plugin (default since k8s 1.17) and audit logs on kubelet access.
• Rotate any credential that has held this permission.

risk_findings.title: Identity can patch/update Secrets — credential overwrite vector
risk_findings.description: The current identity can patch or update Secrets in 7 namespace(s). An attacker can overwrite secret data values — replacing credentials with attacker-controlled values or inserting malicious content consumed by workloads.
evidence: SSRR: patch/update secrets in "monitoring"
SSRR: patch/update secrets in "prod"
SSRR: patch/update secrets in "attack-lab"
SSRR: patch/update secrets in "default"
SSRR: patch/update secrets in "kube-node-lease"
SSRR: patch/update secrets in "kube-public"
SSRR: patch/update secrets in "kube-system"
risk_findings.mitigation:
• patch/update on secrets is as dangerous as read access — an attacker can overwrite any credential.
• Restrict secret mutation to dedicated secret-management service accounts only.
• Enable audit logging for secret patch/update and alert immediately.
• Consider using external secret stores where mutation is controlled by the store's own ACL.

risk_findings.title: Containers with dangerous Linux capabilities detected
risk_findings.description: 1 workload(s)/pod(s) grant dangerous Linux capabilities (SYS_ADMIN, NET_ADMIN, SYS_PTRACE, SYS_MODULE, or DAC_READ_SEARCH). These capabilities enable container escape, kernel module injection, and process introspection.
evidence: Pod monitoring/log-collector has dangerous caps in containers: [collector]
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Remove SYS_ADMIN, NET_ADMIN, SYS_PTRACE, SYS_MODULE, and DAC_READ_SEARCH from container capability sets.
• SYS_ADMIN is nearly equivalent to running privileged — it enables mounting, namespace manipulation, and more.
• SYS_MODULE allows loading kernel modules, enabling rootkits.
• Apply Pod Security Admission 'restricted' profile which drops all capabilities.
• Use seccomp profiles to restrict syscalls even when capabilities are set.

risk_findings.title: Identity can create DaemonSets — cluster-wide code execution
risk_findings.description: The current identity can create DaemonSets. A DaemonSet schedules a pod on every node simultaneously. An attacker can create a DaemonSet with a privileged container or hostPath mount to achieve node-level code execution across the entire cluster.
evidence: SSRR: create daemonsets in "kube-node-lease"
SSRR: create daemonsets in "kube-public"
SSRR: create daemonsets in "kube-system"
SSRR: create daemonsets in "monitoring"
SSRR: create daemonsets in "prod"
SSRR: create daemonsets in "attack-lab"
SSRR: create daemonsets in "default"
risk_findings.mitigation:
• DaemonSets schedule pods on every node — creating one is equivalent to cluster-wide code execution.
• Restrict daemonsets create to cluster administrators only.
• Use admission webhooks (OPA/Gatekeeper, Kyverno) to enforce Pod Security Standards on new DaemonSets.
• Audit all DaemonSet creations and alert on any created outside standard CI/CD pipelines.

risk_findings.title: Identity can patch ServiceAccounts — workload identity injection
risk_findings.description: The current identity can patch ServiceAccounts in 7 namespace(s). An attacker can inject IRSA/Azure Workload Identity/GKE Workload Identity annotations, granting pods using that SA elevated cloud IAM permissions.
evidence: SSRR: patch/update serviceaccounts in "monitoring"
SSRR: patch/update serviceaccounts in "prod"
SSRR: patch/update serviceaccounts in "attack-lab"
SSRR: patch/update serviceaccounts in "default"
SSRR: patch/update serviceaccounts in "kube-node-lease"
SSRR: patch/update serviceaccounts in "kube-public"
SSRR: patch/update serviceaccounts in "kube-system"
risk_findings.mitigation:
• patch/update on serviceaccounts allows adding IRSA/Workload Identity annotations to any SA.
• An attacker can annotate any SA with a high-privilege cloud IAM role ARN, then pods using that SA gain cloud access.
• Restrict serviceaccount mutation to dedicated identity management accounts.
• Enable audit logging and alert on annotation changes to service accounts.

risk_findings.title: Identity can watch Secrets — continuous credential streaming
risk_findings.description: The current identity can watch Secrets in 7 namespace(s). The watch verb opens a streaming connection that delivers every secret create/update event, providing continuous access to all credential changes.
evidence: SSRR: watch secrets in "attack-lab"
SSRR: watch secrets in "default"
SSRR: watch secrets in "kube-node-lease"
SSRR: watch secrets in "kube-public"
SSRR: watch secrets in "kube-system"
SSRR: watch secrets in "monitoring"
SSRR: watch secrets in "prod"
risk_findings.mitigation:
• watch on secrets streams all secret creation and update events in real time.
• This is equivalent to persistent read access — an attacker gets every secret change automatically.
• Revoke watch permission on secrets unless strictly required for a controller.
• Enable audit logging for secret watch events and alert on unexpected principals.

risk_findings.title: Identity can patch/update Nodes — label manipulation and scheduling attacks
risk_findings.description: The current identity can patch or update Node objects. An attacker can manipulate node labels to influence pod scheduling, remove protective taints (e.g., NoSchedule), or corrupt node status information used by the scheduler and controllers.
evidence: SSRR: patch/update nodes
SSRR: patch/update nodes
SSRR: patch/update nodes
SSRR: patch/update nodes
SSRR: patch/update nodes
SSRR: patch/update nodes
SSRR: patch/update nodes
risk_findings.mitigation:
• patch/update on nodes allows modifying labels and taints, influencing pod scheduling.
• An attacker can remove NoSchedule taints from sensitive nodes or add labels to steer workloads.
• Restrict node mutation to system:nodes group and dedicated node management controllers.
• Monitor for unexpected node label or taint changes via audit logging.

risk_findings.title: Identity can patch/update StatefulSets — persistent volume access + SA lateral movement
risk_findings.description: The current identity can patch or update StatefulSets. An attacker can modify the pod template to inject a malicious container or change the service account, gaining access to persistent volumes and the permissions of the patched StatefulSet's SA.
evidence: SSRR: patch/update statefulsets in "attack-lab"
SSRR: patch/update statefulsets in "default"
SSRR: patch/update statefulsets in "kube-node-lease"
SSRR: patch/update statefulsets in "kube-public"
SSRR: patch/update statefulsets in "kube-system"
SSRR: patch/update statefulsets in "monitoring"
SSRR: patch/update statefulsets in "prod"
risk_findings.mitigation:
• patch/update on statefulsets enables injecting new containers, changing the service account, or modifying volume mounts.
• StatefulSets use PersistentVolumes — a patched container can access all existing persistent data.
• Restrict statefulset mutation to dedicated deployment accounts.
• Use admission webhooks to block SA changes in StatefulSet patches.

risk_findings.title: Plaintext secret hardcoded in pod/workload environment variable
risk_findings.description: 1 workload(s)/pod(s) contain plaintext secrets hardcoded in environment variables. These values are visible to anyone with read access to pod specs, stored unencrypted in etcd, and may appear in audit logs.
evidence: Pod attack-lab/dev-pod [dev] env DATABASE_URL (pattern: "database_url"): postgresql://dev:devpass@db.dev.internal...
Pod attack-lab/dev-pod [dev] env GITHUB_TOKEN (pattern: "token"): ghp_fake_github_personal_access_token_12...
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Move secret values into Kubernetes Secrets and reference via secretKeyRef or envFrom.
• Use an external secret store (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, ESO) to eliminate hardcoded values.
• Scan pod specs in CI/CD with tools like detect-secrets or trivy to prevent future occurrences.
• Rotate any exposed credentials immediately.

risk_findings.title: FOOTHOLD CHAIN: rbac-manager → sa:attack-lab:binding-creator-sa → role escalate → cluster_admin (4-hop)
risk_findings.description:
Foothold: Pod (attack-lab/rbac-manager)

pod:attack-lab:rbac-manager (Pod) → [runs_as] → sa:attack-lab:binding-creator-sa (ServiceAccount) → [granted_by] → crb:binding-creator-crb (ClusterRoleBinding) → [bound_to] → clusterrole:binding-creator (ClusterRole) → [can_escalate] → clusterrole:cluster-admin (ClusterRole)

Target: cluster_admin — clusterrole:cluster-admin — full cluster control via built-in cluster-admin role
evidence: pod:attack-lab:rbac-manager --(runs_as: Pod runs as ServiceAccount)--> sa:attack-lab:binding-creator-sa
sa:attack-lab:binding-creator-sa --(granted_by: sa:attack-lab:binding-creator-sa is subject of ClusterRoleBinding "binding-creator-crb")--> crb:binding-creator-crb
crb:binding-creator-crb --(bound_to: ClusterRoleBinding → role)--> clusterrole:binding-creator
clusterrole:binding-creator --(can_escalate: clusterrole:binding-creator grants escalate/bind on clusterroles — cluster-admin path)--> clusterrole:cluster-admin
risk_findings.mitigation: Break the attack path by removing at least one edge. Review permissions and workload configurations along: pod:attack-lab:rbac-manager (Pod) → [runs_as] → sa:attack-lab:binding-creator-sa (ServiceAccount) → [granted_by] → crb:binding-creator-crb (ClusterRoleBinding) → [bound_to] → clusterrole:binding-creator (ClusterRole) → [can_escalate] → clusterrole:cluster-admin (ClusterRole)

edge.reason: clusterrole:binding-creator grants escalate/bind on clusterroles — cluster-admin path
